([/ˈpætwɑː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); locally rendered **Jamaican** **Patwah** and called **Jamaican Creole** by linguists) is an [English-based creole language](/wiki/English-based_creole_language "English-based creole language") mixed heavily with [West African](/wiki/Niger%E2%80%93Congo_languages "Niger–Congo languages") languages, [Arawak](/wiki/Arawak_language "Arawak language"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") and other languages, spoken primarily in [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") and among the [Jamaican diaspora](/wiki/Jamaican_diaspora "Jamaican diaspora"). Words or slang from Jamaican Patois can be heard in other Caribbean countries, the United Kingdom (especially in [London](/wiki/London "London"), where it has heavily influenced [Multicultural London English](/wiki/Multicultural_London_English "Multicultural London English")), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami") in the United States, and [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), Canada.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) It is spoken by most Jamaicans as their [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language").

Female patois speaker saying two sentences



A Jamaican Patois speaker discussing the usage of the language

**Jamaican Patois** ([/ˈpætwɑː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); locally rendered **Jamaican** **Patwah** and called **Jamaican Creole** by linguists) is an [English-based creole language](/wiki/English-based_creole_language "English-based creole language") mixed heavily with [West African](/wiki/Niger%E2%80%93Congo_languages "Niger–Congo languages") languages, [Arawak](/wiki/Arawak_language "Arawak language"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") and other languages, spoken primarily in [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") and among the [Jamaican diaspora](/wiki/Jamaican_diaspora "Jamaican diaspora"). Words or slang from Jamaican Patois can be heard in other Caribbean countries, the United Kingdom (especially in [London](/wiki/London "London"), where it has heavily influenced [Multicultural London English](/wiki/Multicultural_London_English "Multicultural London English")), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami") in the United States, and [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), Canada.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) It is spoken by most Jamaicans as their [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language").

Patois developed in the 17th century when enslaved people from West and Central Africa were exposed to, learned, and nativised the [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") and [dialectal](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") languages spoken by the slaveholders and overseers: [British English](/wiki/British_English "British English"), [Hiberno-English](/wiki/Hiberno-English "Hiberno-English") and [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language"). Jamaican Creole exists in gradations between more conservative [creole](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language") forms that are not significantly mutually intelligible with English,[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown-Blake200832-6) and forms virtually identical to [Standard English](/wiki/Standard_English "Standard English").[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7)

Jamaicans refer to their language as *[Patois](/wiki/Patois "Patois")*, a term also used as a lower-case noun as a multi-use description of [pidgins](/wiki/Pidgin "Pidgin"), creoles, [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect"), and [vernaculars](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") worldwide. Creoles, including Jamaican Patois, are often stigmatised as low-[prestige](/wiki/Prestige_(sociolinguistics) "Prestige (sociolinguistics)") languages even when spoken as the mother tongue by most of the local population.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelupillai2015481-8) Jamaican pronunciation and vocabulary are significantly different from English despite heavy use of English words or derivatives.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown-Blake2008?-9)

Significant Jamaican Patois-speaking communities exist among Jamaican expatriates and non-Jamaicans[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) in [South Florida](/wiki/South_Florida "South Florida"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut"), [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), coastal [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"), the [Cayman Islands](/wiki/Cayman_Islands "Cayman Islands"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) as well as [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), [London](/wiki/London "London"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), and [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham"). The Cayman Islands in particular have a very large Jamaican Patois-speaking community, with 16.4% of the population conversing in the language.[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") variations are found in [San Andrés y Providencia](/wiki/Archipelago_of_San_Andr%C3%A9s,_Providencia_and_Santa_Catalina "Archipelago of San Andrés, Providencia and Santa Catalina") Islands, Colombia,[[13]](#cite_note-13) [Limón Province](/wiki/Lim%C3%B3n_Province "Limón Province"), Costa Rica,[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Colón Province](/wiki/Col%C3%B3n_Province "Colón Province") and [Bocas del Toro Province](/wiki/Bocas_del_Toro_Province "Bocas del Toro Province"), Panama,[[15]](#cite_note-:colon-15) the [Miskito Coast](/wiki/Miskito_Coast "Miskito Coast") of Honduras[[16]](#cite_note-:honwi-16)[[17]](#cite_note-NEGROS_INGLESES-17) and Nicaragua,[[18]](#cite_note-:janic-18) and Belize[[19]](#cite_note-:baymen-19)— brought by enslaved and emancipated Afro-Jamaicans,[[20]](#cite_note-:jahon-20) Creole Jamaicans[[21]](#cite_note-:jacreolenic-21) and Jewish-Jamaican merchants,[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-:jajews-23) as well as, labourers who migrated to work in agriculture (mainly banana production and lumbering), and later to build the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal"), railroads and ports, between the mid-17th and 20th centuries.[[24]](#cite_note-britwi-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Mesolectal forms](/wiki/Post-creole_continuum#Stratification "Post-creole continuum") are similar to very basilectal [Belizean Kriol](/wiki/Belizean_Creole "Belizean Creole").

Jamaican Patois exists mainly as a [spoken language](/wiki/Spoken_language "Spoken language") and is also heavily used for musical purposes, especially in [mento](/wiki/Mento "Mento"), [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") and [dancehall](/wiki/Dancehall "Dancehall") as well as other genres. Although standard British English is used for most writing in Jamaica, Jamaican Patois has gained ground as a [literary language](/wiki/Literary_language "Literary language") for almost a hundred years. [Claude McKay](/wiki/Claude_McKay "Claude McKay") published his book of Jamaican poems *[Songs of Jamaica](/wiki/Songs_of_Jamaica "Songs of Jamaica")* in 1912. Patois and English are frequently used for stylistic contrast ([codeswitching](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching")) in new forms of Internet writing.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jamaican_Patois&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Phonology")]

Accounts of [basilectal](/wiki/Basilectal "Basilectal") Jamaican Patois (that is, its most divergent rural varieties) suggest around 21 [phonemic](/wiki/Phonemic "Phonemic") [consonants](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant")[[27]](#cite_note-Devonish_2004_456-27) with an additional phoneme (/h/) in the Western dialect.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelupillai2015483-28) There are between nine and sixteen [vowels](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel").[[29]](#cite_note-29) Some vowels are capable of nasalisation and others can be lengthened.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelupillai2015483-28)

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

|  | |  | [l](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_lateral_approximant "Voiced alveolar lateral approximant") |  | |  | |  | |  | |

:   **[^1](#ref_1)** The status of /h/ as a phoneme is dialectal: in western varieties, it is a full phoneme and there are minimal pairs (/hiit/ 'hit' and /iit/ 'eat'); in central and eastern varieties, vowel-initial words take an initial [h] after vowel-final words, preventing the two vowels from falling together, so that the words for 'hand' and 'and' (both underlyingly /an/) may be pronounced [han] or [an].[[31]](#cite_note-31)
:   **[^2](#ref_2)** The palatal stops [c], [ɟ][[note 1]](#cite_note-32) and [ɲ] are considered phonemic by some accounts[[32]](#cite_note-33) and [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic "Phonetic") by others.[[33]](#cite_note-34) For the latter interpretation, their appearance is included in the larger phenomenon of phonetic [palatalisation](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)").

Examples of palatalisation include:[[34]](#cite_note-35)

* /kiuu/ → [ciuː] → [cuː] ('a quarter quart (of rum)')
* /ɡiaad/ → [ɟiaːd] → [ɟaːd] ('guard')
* /piaa + piaa/ → [pʲiãːpʲiãː] → [pʲãːpʲãː] ('weak')

Voiced stops are [implosive](/wiki/Implosive_consonant "Implosive consonant") whenever in the onset of prominent syllables (especially word-initially) so that /biit/ ('beat') is pronounced [ɓiːt] and /ɡuud/ ('good') as [ɠuːd].[[27]](#cite_note-Devonish_2004_456-27)

Before a syllabic /l/, the contrast between [alveolar](/wiki/Alveolar_consonant "Alveolar consonant") and [velar](/wiki/Velar_consonant "Velar consonant") consonants has been historically neutralised with alveolar consonants becoming velar so that the word for 'bottle' is /bakl̩/ and the word for 'idle' is /aiɡl̩/.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Jamaican_Creole_vowel_chart.svg/250px-Jamaican_Creole_vowel_chart.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Jamaican_Creole_vowel_chart.svg)

[Vowels](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel") of Jamaican Patois. from [Harry (2006](#CITEREFHarry2006):128)

Jamaican Patois exhibits two types of [vowel harmony](/wiki/Vowel_harmony "Vowel harmony"); peripheral vowel harmony, wherein only sequences of peripheral vowels (that is, /i/, /u/, and /a/) can occur within a syllable; and back harmony, wherein /i/ and /u/ cannot occur within a syllable together (that is, /uu/ and /ii/ are allowed but \* /ui/ and \* /iu/ are not).[[36]](#cite_note-37) These two phenomena account for three long vowels and four [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong"):[[37]](#cite_note-38)

| Vowel | Example | Gloss |
| --- | --- | --- |
| /ii/ | /biini/ | 'tiny' |
| /aa/ | /baaba/ | 'barber' |
| /uu/ | /buut/ | 'booth' |
| /ia/ | /biak/ | 'bake' |
| /ai/ | /baik/ | 'bike' |
| /ua/ | /buat/ | 'boat' |
| /au/ | /taun/ | 'town' |

## Sociolinguistic variation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jamaican_Patois&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Sociolinguistic variation")]

Jamaican Patois features a [creole continuum](/wiki/Post-creole_speech_continuum "Post-creole speech continuum") (or a *linguistic continuum*):[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41) the variety of the language closest to the [lexifier](/wiki/Lexifier "Lexifier") language (the [acrolect](/wiki/Acrolect "Acrolect")) cannot be distinguished systematically from intermediate varieties (collectively referred to as the [mesolect](/wiki/Mesolect "Mesolect")) or even from the most divergent rural varieties (collectively referred to as the [basilect](/wiki/Basilect "Basilect")).[[41]](#cite_note-irvinesobers-42) This situation came about with contact between speakers of a number of [Niger–Congo languages](/wiki/Niger%E2%80%93Congo_languages "Niger–Congo languages") and various dialects of English, the latter of which were all perceived as prestigious and whose use carried socio-economic benefits.[[42]](#cite_note-43) The span of a speaker's command of the continuum generally corresponds to social context.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jamaican_Patois&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Grammar")]

The tense/aspect system of Jamaican Patois is fundamentally unlike that of English. There are no [morphologically](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)") marked past [participles](/wiki/Participle "Participle"); instead, two different participle words exist: *en* and *a*. These are not [verbs](/wiki/Verb "Verb"), but rather invariant [particles](/wiki/Grammatical_particle "Grammatical particle") that cannot stand alone (like the English *to be*). Their function also differs from those of English.

According to Bailey (1966), the progressive category is marked by /a~da~de/. Alleyne (1980) claims that /a~da/ marks the progressive and that the habitual aspect is unmarked but by its accompaniment with words such as "always", "usually", etc. (i.e. is absent as a grammatical category). Mufwene (1984) and Gibson and Levy (1984) propose a past-only habitual category marked by /juusta/ as in /weɹ wi juusta liv iz not az kual az iiɹ/ ('where we used to live is not as cold as here').[[44]](#cite_note-45)

For the present tense, an uninflected verb combining with an iterative adverb marks habitual meaning as in /tam aawez nua wen kieti tel pan im/ ('Tom always knows when Katy tells/has told about him').[[45]](#cite_note-46)

* *en* is a tense indicator
* *a* is an aspect marker
* *(a) go* is used to indicate the future
* **Mi run** (/mi ɹon/)
  + I run (habitually); I ran
* **Mi a run** or **Mi de run**, (/mi a ɹon/ *or* /mi de ɹon/)
  + I am running
* **A run mi did a run**, (/a ɹon mi dida ɹon/ *or* /a ɹon mi ben(w)en a ɹon/)
  + I was running
* **Mi did run** (/mi did ɹon/ *or* /mi ben(w)en ɹon/)
  + I have run; I had run
* **Mi a go run** (/mi a ɡo ɹon/)
  + I am going to run; I will go on a run

As in other Caribbean Creoles (that is, [Guyanese Creole](/wiki/Guyanese_Creole "Guyanese Creole") and [San Andrés-Providencia Creole](/wiki/San_Andr%C3%A9s-Providencia_Creole "San Andrés-Providencia Creole"); [Sranan Tongo](/wiki/Sranan_Tongo "Sranan Tongo") is excluded) /fi/ has a number of functions, including:[[46]](#cite_note-47)

* Directional, dative, or benefactive preposition
  + ***Dem a fight fi wi*** (/dem a fait fi wi/) ('They are fighting for us')[[47]](#cite_note-48)
* [Genitive](/wiki/Genitive "Genitive") preposition (that is, marker of possession)
  + ***Dat a fi mi book*** (/dat a fi mi buk/) ('that's my book')
* Modal auxiliary expressing obligation or futurity
  + ***Him fi kom up ya*** (/im fi kom op ja/) ('he ought to come up here')
* Pre-infinitive complementiser
  + ***Unu haffi kiip sumting far di guinea people-dem fi biit dem muzik*** (/unu hafi kiip samtiŋ faɹ de ɡini piipl-dem fi biit dem miuzik/) ('you have to contribute something to the [Guinean](/wiki/Guinea "Guinea") [People](/wiki/Demographics_of_Guinea "Demographics of Guinea") for playing their music')[[48]](#cite_note-49)

### Pronominal system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jamaican_Patois&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Pronominal system")]

The [pronominal](/wiki/Pronominal "Pronominal") system of Standard English has a four-way distinction of [person](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person"), [number](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number"), [gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") and [case](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case"). Some varieties of Jamaican Patois do not have the gender or case distinction, but all varieties distinguish between the second person singular and plural (you).[[49]](#cite_note-50)

* I, me = /mi/
* you, you (singular) = /ju/
* you (plural) = /unu/
* he, him = /im/ (pronounced [ĩ] in the [basilect](/wiki/Basilect "Basilect") varieties)
* she, her = /ʃi/ *or* /im/ (no gender distinction in basilect varieties; /im/ refers to both)
* we, us, our = /wi/
* they, them, their = /dem/

### Copula

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jamaican_Patois&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Copula")]

* the Jamaican Patois [equative](/wiki/Equative_sentence "Equative sentence") verb is also *a*
  + e.g. /mi a di tiitʃa/ ('I am the teacher')
* Jamaican Patois has a separate locative verb *deh*
  + e.g. /wi de a london/ *or* /wi de inna london/ ('we are in London')
* with true adjectives in Jamaican Patois, no copula is needed
  + e.g. /mi haadbak nau/ ('I am old now')

This is akin to Spanish in that both have two distinct forms of the verb "to be" – *ser* and *estar* – in which *ser* is equative and *estar* is locative. Other languages, such as Portuguese and Italian, make a similar distinction (see also: [Romance Copula](/wiki/Romance_copula "Romance copula")).

### Negation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jamaican_Patois&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Negation")]

* /no/ is used as a present tense negator:
  + /if kau no did nua au im tɹuatual tan im udn tʃaans pieɹsiid/ ('If the cow knew that his throat wasn't capable of swallowing a pear seed, he wouldn't have swallowed it')[[50]](#cite_note-51)
* /kiaan/ is used in the same way as English *can't*
  + /it a puaɹ tiŋ dat kiaan maʃ ant/ ('It is a poor thing that can't mash an ant')[[51]](#cite_note-52)
* /neva/ is a negative past participle.[[52]](#cite_note-53)
  + /dʒan neva tiif di moni/ ('John did not steal the money')

## Orthography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jamaican_Patois&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Orthography")]

Main article: [Cassidy/JLU orthography](/wiki/Cassidy/JLU_orthography "Cassidy/JLU orthography")

Patois has long been written with various respellings compared to English so that, for example, the word "there" might be written ⟨de⟩, ⟨deh⟩, or ⟨dere⟩, and the word "three" as ⟨tree⟩, ⟨tri⟩, or ⟨trii⟩. Standard English spelling is often used and a nonstandard spelling sometimes becomes widespread even though it is neither phonetic nor standard (e.g. ⟨pickney⟩ for /pikni/, 'child').

In 2002, the Jamaican Language Unit was set up at the [University of the West Indies](/wiki/University_of_the_West_Indies "University of the West Indies") at Mona to begin standardising the language, with the aim of supporting non-English-speaking Jamaicans according to their constitutional guarantees of equal rights, as services of the state are normally provided in English, which a significant portion of the population cannot speak fluently. The vast majority of such persons are speakers of Jamaican Patois. It was argued that failure to provide services of the state in a language in such general use or discriminatory treatment by officers of the state based on the inability of a citizen to use English violates the rights of citizens. The proposal was made that freedom from discrimination on the ground of language be inserted into the Charter of Rights.[[53]](#cite_note-54) They standardised the Jamaican alphabet as follows:[[54]](#cite_note-55)

Short vowels

| Letter | Patois | English |
| --- | --- | --- |
| i | sik | sick |
| e | bel | bell |
| a | ban | band |
| o | kot | cut |
| u | kuk | cook |

Long vowels

| Letter | Patois | English |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ii | tii | tea |
| aa | baal | ball |
| uu | shuut | shoot |

Diphthongs

| Letter | Patois | English |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ie | kiek | cake |
| uo | gruo | grow |
| ai | bait | bite |
| ou | kou | cow |

Nasal vowels are written with *-hn*, as in *kyaahn* (can't) and *iihn* (isn't it?)

Consonants

| Letter | Patois | English |
| --- | --- | --- |
| b | biek | bake |
| d | daag | dog |
| ch | choch | church |
| f | fuud | food |
| g | guot | goat |
| h | hen | hen |
| j | joj | judge |
| k | kait | kite |
| l | liin | lean |
| m | man | man |
| n | nais | nice |
| ng | sing | sing |
| p | piil | peel |
| r | ron | run |
| s | sik | sick |
| sh | shout | shout |
| t | tuu | two |
| v | vuot | vote |
| w | wail | wild |
| y | yong | young |
| z | zuu | zoo |
| zh | vorzhan | version |

*h* is written according to local pronunciation, so that *hen* (hen) and *en* (end) are distinguished in writing for speakers of western Jamaican, but not for those of central Jamaican.

## Vocabulary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jamaican_Patois&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Vocabulary")]

See also: [List of African words in Jamaican Patois](/wiki/List_of_African_words_in_Jamaican_Patois "List of African words in Jamaican Patois")

Jamaican Patois contains many [loanwords](/wiki/Loanwords "Loanwords"), some of which are African in origin, primarily from [Twi](/wiki/Twi "Twi") (a dialect of [Akan](/wiki/Akan_language "Akan language")).[[55]](#cite_note-Library_of_Alexandria-56) Many loanwords come from English, but some are also borrowed from [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"), [Arawak](/wiki/Arawakan_languages "Arawakan languages"), as well as, Scottish and [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language") dialects.

Examples from African languages include /se/ meaning *that* (in the sense of "he told me that..." = /im tel mi se/), taken from [Ashanti](/wiki/Ashanti_language "Ashanti language") [Twi](/wiki/Twi "Twi"), and [duppy](/wiki/Duppy "Duppy") meaning *ghost*, taken from the Twi word *dupon* ('cotton tree root'), because of the African belief of malicious spirits originating in the roots of trees (in Jamaica and Ghana, particularly the cotton tree known in both places as "Odom").[[56]](#cite_note-57) The pronoun /unu/, used for the plural form of *you*, is taken from the [Igbo language](/wiki/Igbo_language "Igbo language"). *Red eboe* describes a fair-skinned black person because of the reported account of fair skin among the [Igbo](/wiki/Igbo_people "Igbo people") in the mid-1700s.[[57]](#cite_note-58) *De* meaning *to be* (at a location) comes from [Yoruba](/wiki/Yoruba_language "Yoruba language").[[58]](#cite_note-59) From the Ashanti-Akan, comes the term *Obeah* which means witchcraft, from the Ashanti Twi word *Ɔbayi* which also means "witchcraft".[[55]](#cite_note-Library_of_Alexandria-56)

Words from Hindi include *[ganja](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)")* (marijuana).[[59]](#cite_note-60) *Pickney* or *pickiney* meaning child, taken from an earlier form (*[piccaninny](/wiki/Pickaninny "Pickaninny")*) was ultimately borrowed from the Portuguese *pequenino* (the diminutive of *pequeno*, small) or Spanish *pequeño* ('small').[[60]](#cite_note-61) There are many words referring to popular produce, food items, and [Jamaican cuisine](/wiki/Jamaican_cuisine "Jamaican cuisine")—*[ackee](/wiki/Ackee "Ackee")*, *[callaloo](/wiki/Callaloo "Callaloo")*, *[guinep](/wiki/Melicoccus_bijugatus "Melicoccus bijugatus")*, *bammy*, *[roti](/wiki/Roti "Roti")*, *[dal](/wiki/Dal "Dal")*, *kamranga*.

## Literature and film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jamaican_Patois&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Literature and film")]

A rich body of literature has developed in Jamaican Patois. Notable among early authors and works are [Thomas MacDermot](/wiki/Thomas_MacDermot "Thomas MacDermot")'s *All Jamaica Library* and [Claude McKay](/wiki/Claude_McKay "Claude McKay")'s *[Songs of Jamaica](/wiki/Songs_of_Jamaica "Songs of Jamaica")* (1909), and, more recently, [dub poets](/wiki/Dub_poetry "Dub poetry") [Linton Kwesi Johnson](/wiki/Linton_Kwesi_Johnson "Linton Kwesi Johnson") and [Mikey Smith](/wiki/Mikey_Smith "Mikey Smith"). Subsequently, the life-work of [Louise Bennett](/wiki/Louise_Bennett "Louise Bennett") or Miss Lou (1919–2006) is particularly notable for her use of the rich colourful patois, despite being shunned by traditional literary groups. "The Jamaican Poetry League excluded her from its meetings, and editors failed to include her in anthologies."[[61]](#cite_note-62) Nonetheless, she argued forcefully for the recognition of Jamaican as a full language, with the same pedigree as the dialect from which [Standard English](/wiki/Standard_English "Standard English") had sprung:

> Dah language weh yuh proud a,  
> Weh yuh honour an respec –  
> Po Mas Charlie, yuh no know se  
> Dat it spring from dialec!

— *Bans a Killin*

After the 1960s, the status of Jamaican Patois rose as a number of respected linguistic studies were published, by [Frederic Cassidy](/wiki/Frederic_G._Cassidy "Frederic G. Cassidy") (1961, 1967), Bailey (1966) and others.[[62]](#cite_note-63) Subsequently, it has gradually become mainstream to codemix or write complete pieces in Jamaican Patois; proponents include [Kamau Brathwaite](/wiki/Kamau_Brathwaite "Kamau Brathwaite"), who also analyses the position of Creole poetry in his *History of the Voice: The Development of Nation Language in Anglophone Caribbean Poetry* (1984). However, Standard English remains the more prestigious literary medium in [Jamaican literature](/wiki/Jamaican_literature "Jamaican literature"). Canadian-Caribbean science-fiction novelist [Nalo Hopkinson](/wiki/Nalo_Hopkinson "Nalo Hopkinson") often writes in Trinidadian and sometimes Jamaican Patois. [Jean D'Costa](/wiki/Jean_D%27Costa "Jean D'Costa") penned a series of popular children's novels, including *[Sprat Morrison](/wiki/Sprat_Morrison "Sprat Morrison")* (1972; 1990), *[Escape to Last Man Peak](/wiki/Escape_to_Last_Man_Peak "Escape to Last Man Peak")* (1976), and *Voice in the Wind* (1978), which draw liberally from Jamaican Patois for dialogue, while presenting narrative prose in Standard English.[[63]](#cite_note-64) [Marlon James](/wiki/Marlon_James_(novelist) "Marlon James (novelist)") employs Patois in his novels including *[A Brief History of Seven Killings](/wiki/A_Brief_History_of_Seven_Killings "A Brief History of Seven Killings")* (2014). In his science fiction novel *Kaya Abaniah and the Father of the Forest* (2015), British-Trinidadian author Wayne Gerard Trotman presents dialogue in [Trinidadian Creole](/wiki/Trinidadian_Creole "Trinidadian Creole"), Jamaican Patois, and French while employing Standard English for narrative prose.

Jamaican Patois is also presented in some films and other media, such as for example the character [Tia Dalma](/wiki/Tia_Dalma "Tia Dalma")'s speech from *[Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest](/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean:_Dead_Man%27s_Chest "Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest")*, and a few scenes in *[Meet Joe Black](/wiki/Meet_Joe_Black "Meet Joe Black")* in which [Brad Pitt](/wiki/Brad_Pitt "Brad Pitt")'s character converses with a Jamaican woman ([Lois Kelly Miller](/wiki/Lois_Kelly_Miller "Lois Kelly Miller")). In addition, early Jamaican films like *[The Harder They Come](/wiki/The_Harder_They_Come "The Harder They Come")* (1972), *[Rockers](/wiki/Rockers_(1978_film) "Rockers (1978 film)")* (1978), and many of the films produced by [Palm Pictures](/wiki/Palm_Pictures "Palm Pictures") in the mid-1990s (e.g. *[Dancehall Queen](/wiki/Dancehall_Queen "Dancehall Queen")* and *[Third World Cop](/wiki/Third_World_Cop "Third World Cop")*) have most of their dialogue in Jamaican Patois; some of these films have even been subtitled in English. It was also used in the second season of *[Marvel's Luke Cage](/wiki/Luke_Cage_(TV_series) "Luke Cage (TV series)")* but the accents were described as "awful" by Jamaican Americans.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

### Bible

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jamaican_Patois&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Bible")]

In December 2011, it was reported that the Bible was being translated into Jamaican Patois. The [Gospel of Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") has already appeared as *[Jiizas: di Buk We Luuk Rait bout Im](/wiki/Jiizas:_di_Buk_We_Luuk_Rait_bout_Im "Jiizas: di Buk We Luuk Rait bout Im")*. While the Rev. Courtney Stewart, managing the translation as General Secretary of the West Indies Bible Society, believes this will help elevate the status of Jamaican Patois, others think that such a move would undermine efforts at promoting the use of English.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Patois New Testament was launched in Britain (where the Jamaican diaspora is significant) in October 2012 as "[Di Jamiekan Nyuu Testiment](/wiki/Di_Jamiekan_Nyuu_Testiment "Di Jamiekan Nyuu Testiment")", and with print and audio versions in Jamaica in December 2012.[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68)

A comparison of the [Lord's Prayer](/wiki/Lord%27s_Prayer "Lord's Prayer")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| ...as it occurs in *Di Jamiekan Nyuu Testiment*:[[68]](#cite_note-69)  Wi Faada we iina evn,  mek piipl av nof rispek fi yu an yu niem.  Mek di taim kom wen yu ruul iina evri wie.  Mek we yu waahn apm pan ort apm,  jos laik ou a wa yu waahn fi apm iina evn apm  Tide gi wi di fuud we wi niid.  Paadn wi fi aal a di rang we wi du,  siem laik ou wi paadn dem we du wi rang.  An no mek wi fies notn we wi kaaz wi fi sin,  bot protek wi fram di wikid wan. | ...as it occurs in [English Standard Version](/wiki/English_Standard_Version "English Standard Version"):  Our Father in heaven,  hallowed be Your name.  Your kingdom come,  Your will be done,  on earth, as it is in heaven.  Give us this day our daily bread,  and forgive us our debts,  as we also have forgiven our debtors.  And lead us not into temptation,  but deliver us from evil.[[69]](#cite_note-70) |

The system of spelling used in *Di Jamiekan Nyuu Testiment* is the phonetic Cassidy Writing system adopted by the Jamaica Language Unit of the University of the West Indies, and while most Jamaicans use the informal "Miss Lou" writing system, the Cassidy Writing system is an effort at standardising Patois in its written form.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

### Recent developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jamaican_Patois&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Recent developments")]

Jamaican Patois has made some major strides in recent years, becoming one of the languages available on Google Translate and being used by the People's National Party (PNP), which released an audio version of its 2025 general election manifesto in the language.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)[[73]](#cite_note-74)[[74]](#cite_note-75)

## Sample text

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jamaican_Patois&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Sample text")]

Article 1 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights") in Jamaican Patois:

> *Evribadi baan frii an iikwal wid rispek fi demself (digniti) an wid raits. Dem nuo ou fi riizn kaa dem av sens an nuo we rait fram we rang (kanshenz) an dem fi chriit dem wan aneda laik se dem a breda an sista.*[[75]](#cite_note-76)

Article 1 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights") in English:

> All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood.
